_description: Symfony exposure patterns derived from three real-world security reports (see docs/threat-intel/). Each `signal` is a passive probe; each `finding_type` becomes a HunterPy finding. The intent is that ANY new module can consume this JSON without code changes.

exposure_paths.title: Symfony Profiler exposed in production
exposure_paths.description: The Symfony Debug Profiler is publicly reachable. Exposes environment variables (DATABASE_URL, APP_SECRET, OAuth secrets), session data, request parameters, and full server configuration. Source: SECREP1 + SECREP2.

exposure_paths.title: Symfony Profiler exposes phpinfo()
exposure_paths.description: phpinfo() served via the profiler. Discloses PHP version (often EOL), loaded modules, INI settings, and every environment variable — including DATABASE_URL, APP_SECRET, OAUTH_AZURE_CLIENT_SECRET, API credentials. Source: SECREP2.

exposure_paths.title: Symfony Profiler arbitrary file read
exposure_paths.description: /_profiler/open?file=… serves any file path the web user can read. Confirmed reachable by reading config/packages/security.yaml. From there an attacker can read .env, doctrine.yaml, services.yaml, and the entire src/ tree. Source: SECREP2.

exposure_paths.title: Symfony Profiler search UI exposed
exposure_paths.description: Allows enumerating profiler tokens for every recorded request. Useful for replaying past requests including authenticated ones. Source: SECREP2.

exposure_paths.title: Symfony 1.x/2.x dev front controller reachable
exposure_paths.description: app_dev.php is the dev-mode front controller. Its presence in production almost always means the profiler + verbose errors are exposed and `?+--env=dev` / `?XDEBUG_SESSION_START` query tricks may apply. Source: SECREP-symfony-upstream-issues (Medium article).

exposure_paths.title: Legacy Symfony profiler reachable via app_dev.php
exposure_paths.description: Mirror of the modern profiler exposure, available via the legacy front controller. Source: SECREP-symfony-upstream-issues.

exposure_paths.title: Legacy Symfony parameters.yml readable
exposure_paths.description: Reading parameters.yml typically leaks database credentials, the application secret token, and SMTP creds. Source: SECREP-symfony-upstream-issues.

exposure_paths.title: Symfony /_fragment endpoint reachable
exposure_paths.description: If FrameworkBundle's fragment listener is exposed without a properly-set fragment secret, this endpoint can be coerced into rendering arbitrary controllers with attacker-supplied arguments.

query_string_tricks.name: app_env_injection
query_string_tricks.description: Symfony Console's ArgvInput reads $_SERVER['argv']. A request like `?+--env=dev` injects an extra CLI argument that flips APP_ENV to dev, which in turn re-enables the profiler and verbose errors on the next request. Source: SECREP-symfony-upstream-issues (Symfony#XXXXX).

query_string_tricks.name: app_debug_injection
query_string_tricks.description: Same family as APP_ENV injection — `?+--no-debug` flips APP_DEBUG. Combined with --env=dev it forces dev+debug mode.

associated_cves.title: Symfony APP_ENV / APP_DEBUG injection via Console runtime
associated_cves.summary: Symfony's ArgvInput reads $_SERVER['argv'], allowing attackers to flip APP_ENV / APP_DEBUG via a crafted query string when the runtime component is misconfigured.

associated_cves.title: Symfony 1 gadget chain via sfNamespacedParameterHolder
associated_cves.summary: If application code calls unserialize() on user input, the sfNamespacedParameterHolder + sfOutputEscaperArrayDecorator chain leads to RCE via call_user_func with shell_exec.

associated_cves.title: ImageTragick — shell injection via crafted image
associated_cves.summary: Multiple coders in ImageMagick interpret SVG/MVG primitives that can shell out via crafted images. Often reachable from any web app that resizes user uploads via the imagick PHP extension. Source: SECREP1.

associated_cves.title: ImageMagick SVG parser hang / DoS
associated_cves.summary: Malformed SVG processing causes infinite loop / DoS in ImageMagick. Source: SECREP1.

associated_cves.title: End-of-life PHP version still in production
associated_cves.summary: PHP 5.x and PHP 7.0-7.3 are EOL — no security patches. Combined with `disable_functions` left empty (exec, system, shell_exec, passthru, proc_open, popen, curl_exec) this is a trivial RCE pivot point. Source: SECREP1.

attack_chain_recipe.name: symfony_full_pwnage
attack_chain_recipe.description: Reproducible chain observed across all three reports.
ordered_steps: 1. Fingerprint: x-debug-token / x-debug-token-link header OR Symfony body markers.
3. Hit /_profiler/phpinfo  → harvest env vars (APP_SECRET, DATABASE_URL, OAuth secrets).
5. Map routes via /_profiler router panel  → locate /admin/* with broken access_control.
6. Pivot: use exposed DB creds, OR exploit any file-upload endpoint surfaced (`institutional[image]`, `blog[image]`).
